coredata.dc:title: A dual reference ion-imprinted ratiometric fluorescence probe for simultaneous detection of silver (I) and lead (II)
coredata.dc:creator: Lu, Hongzhi
Yu, Chunwei
Xu, Shoufang
coredata.dc:description: We describe a dual reference ion-imprinted ratiometric fluorescence probe for simultaneous detection of Ag+ and Pb2+. Carbon dots (CDs) and gold nanoclusters (AuNCs) were adopted for fluorescence signal output. To improve the conversion efficiency from molecular recognition to fluorescence signal output, functional groups on the surface of fluorescence nanoparticles were used to form a chelate with template ions during polymerization. During the detection of Ag+, the CDs were quenched, while AuNCs remained stable as the reference. For Pb2+ detection, AuNCs were the response signal, and CDs were the reference. Under optimal conditions, (ICDs)0/(IAuNCs) displayed a good linear relationship with the concentration of Pb2+ from 50 to 900 nM with detection limits of 26 nM; (IAuNCs)0/(ICDs) had a good linear relationship with the concentration of Ag+ from 0.2–12.5 μM with a detection limit of 86 nM. Using this dual reference strategy, Ag+ and Pb2+ can be detected simultaneously in real water samples with satisfactory recoveries. This work enables high-throughput detection based on fluorescence signal output, which saves detection time and costs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ion imprinted polymers
Ratiometric fluorescence probe, Dual-template imprinting
Lead (II)
Silver (I)

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of dual reference ion imprinted ratiometric fluorescence probes
Preparation of dual reference ion-imprinted ratiometric fluorescence probes
Analytical performance of dual reference ion-imprinted fluorescence polymers
Selectivity studies for Ag+ and Pb2+ detection
Practical applications in real water samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the preparation of dual reference ion-imprinted ratiometric fluorescence probes and the sensing mechanism for Pb2+ and Ag+ detection.

The TEM image of IIP (A) and NIP (B). Insets are the SEM images of the corresponding particles. N2 adsorption isotherms and the pore size distribution for IIP (C), and the fluorescence spectra of IIP (D).

The fluorescence spectra of the IIP (A) and NIP (C) exposure to different concentrations of Ag+; The plot of (I600)0/I430 from IIP (B) or NIP (D) as a function of the Ag+ concentration. The experimental conditions were pH = 7.0 under room temperature; the excitation wavelength was 360 nm, and the slit widths of emission and excitation were 5 nm (n = 3); the concentration of IIP or NIP was 100 mg L−1, and the reaction time was 10 min.

The fluorescence spectra of the IIFP (A) and NIFP (C) fluorescence polymers exposed to different concentrations of Pb2+. The plot of (I430)0/I600 stems from IIP (B) or NIP (D) as a function of the Pb2+ concentration. The experimental conditions were pH = 7.0 under room temperature; excitation wavelength was 360 nm, and the slit widths of emission and excitation were 5 nm (n = 3); the concentrations of IIP or NIP were 100 mg L−1, and the reaction time was 20 min.

(A) The fluorescence spectra of the IIP exposed to different concentrations of Pb2+ and Ag+ simultaneously. (B) The plot of (I430)0/I600 as a function of the Pb2+ concentration. (C) The plot of (I600)0/I430 as a function of the Ag+ concentration. (D) The fluorescence spectra of the NIP exposed to different concentrations of Pb2+ and Ag+ simultaneously. The experimental conditions were pH = 7.0 under room temperature; excitation wavelength was 360 nm, and slit widths of emission and excitation were 5 nm (n = 3); the concentrations of IIP was 100 mg L−1, and the reaction time was 10 min.

The fluorescence spectra of the IIP (A) or NIP (B) exposed to different metal ions (Cu2+, Cd2+, Cr3+, Co2+, Hg2+, Fe3+, Pb2+, or Ag+). (C) The selectivity of IIP and NIP to several metal ions. (D) The competitive experimental of IIP to co-existing two kinds of metal ions. The concentration of Ag+ was 10 μM, that of Pb2+ ions was 2.5 μM, and that of other interference ions was all 20 μM. The experimental conditions were pH = 7.0 under room temperature; excitation wavelength was 360 nm, and slit widths of emission and excitation were 5 nm (n = 3); the concentrations of IIP was 100 mg L−1, and the reaction time was 10 min.
Recoveries of Pb2+ and Ag+ in spiked river and tap water using the as-prepared IIP.

head.ce:title: A dual reference ion-imprinted ratiometric fluorescence probe for simultaneous detection of silver (I) and lead (II)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Linyi University, Linyi, 276005, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Laboratory of Environmental Monitoring, School of Tropical and Laboratory Medicine, Hainan Medical University, Haikou, 571101, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Tropical and Laboratory Medicine

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Laboratory of Functional Polymers, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Linyi University, Linyi, 276005, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We describe a dual reference ion-imprinted ratiometric fluorescence probe for simultaneous detection of Ag+ and Pb2+. Carbon dots (CDs) and gold nanoclusters (AuNCs) were adopted for fluorescence signal output. To improve the conversion efficiency from molecular recognition to fluorescence signal output, functional groups on the surface of fluorescence nanoparticles were used to form a chelate with template ions during polymerization. During the detection of Ag+, the CDs were quenched, while AuNCs remained stable as the reference. For Pb2+ detection, AuNCs were the response signal, and CDs were the reference. Under optimal conditions, (ICDs)0/(IAuNCs) displayed a good linear relationship with the concentration of Pb2+ from 50 to 900 nM with detection limits of 26 nM; (IAuNCs)0/(ICDs) had a good linear relationship with the concentration of Ag+ from 0.2–12.5 μM with a detection limit of 86 nM. Using this dual reference strategy, Ag+ and Pb2+ can be detected simultaneously in real water samples with satisfactory recoveries. This work enables high-throughput detection based on fluorescence signal output, which saves detection time and costs.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, the catalytic degradation [1,2], removal [3] and detection [4–9] of typical pollutants in the water environment has become a research hotspot. Heavy metal ions such as Hg2+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Ag+, and Cu2+ in environmental water can seriously damage animals, plants, and humans. Selective detection of heavy metal ions in environmental samples is particularly important. Currently, several analytical methods have been employed for heavy metal ion determination such as atomic absorption spectrometry [4], inductive coupled plasma mass spectrometry [5], ultraviolet-visible spectrophotometry [6], electrochemical biosensors [7], and fluorescent probes [8,9]. Of these detection methods, fluorescence probes based on fluorescent nanoparticles have attracted much attention due to their sensitivity, simplicity, low background, improved optical properties, and the ability for easy chemical functionalization and modification. Several metal ions can simultaneously quench one kind of fluorescent nanoparticle, and thus the selectivity of the fluorescent probe for detecting metal ions is relatively low. Ion imprinted polymers (IIPs) are a class of polymers with high selectivity for the template (target analytes) [10–12]. Due to their high selectivity and high chemical stability, IIPs are widely used in ion removal, sample pretreatment, and chemical sensors.

Combining the high selectivity of IIPs with the high sensitivity of fluorescence sensors, ion imprinted fluorescence polymers can be widely used in the detection of target ions in complex matrices [13–15]. However, the entire synthesis process for ion imprinted fluorescence polymers including fluorescence nanoparticles synthesis, polymer preparation, and template elution is time-consuming and complex. More importantly, one kind of ion imprinted fluorescence polymers can only detect one target due to selectivity demands. Due to the requirements for various ionic detection in environmental water samples, we expect that simultaneous detection of multiple ions can be achieved by one kind of ion imprinted fluorescence polymers. Thus, detection would save cost and time.

Multi-template imprinting has been explored for high-throughput detection based on sample pretreatment-coupled chromatography detection [16–18]. However, ion imprinted fluorescence polymers are mostly designed for one target due to a lack of separation function. Considering that one signal only responds to one target, simultaneous detection of two different targets can be achieved by two independent fluorescent signals [19,20]. For example, simultaneous detection of cephalexin and ceftriaxone based on molecularly imprinted fluorescence polymers has been reported in which graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and CdTe QDs were quenched by cephalexin and ceftriaxone, respectively [19]. Simultaneous detection of copper ions and mercury ions based on ion imprinted fluorescence polymers in different regions has been reported [15].

In the field of fluorescence probes, ratiometric fluorescent probes have been widely explored due to their high sensitivity [21–26]. Ratiometric fluorescent probes can be classified into two groups: ratiometric fluorescent probes with one reference signal [21–23] and ratiometric fluorescent probes with two reversible signal changes [24–26]. Detection processes based on the reference signal monitor the fluorescence intensity of one fluorophore as it changes relative to the unchanged signal. Inspired by ratiometric fluorescent probes with a reference signal, we expect that dual reference ratiometric fluorescent probes can achieve simultaneous detection of two targets.

As a proof of concept, dual reference ion imprinted ratiometric fluorescence probes for the simultaneous detection of Ag+ and Pb2+ are proposed here. Carbon dots (CDs) and gold nanoclusters (AuNCs) were used to monitor Ag+ and Pb2+, respectively. While maintaining the sensitivity and selectivity of the ion imprinted ratiometric fluorescence probe, the as-prepared dual reference ion imprinted ratiometric fluorescence probe can simultaneously detect Ag+ and Pb2+, which greatly improves the detection efficiency. The Ag+ and Pb2+ can even be detected simultaneously in real water samples with satisfactory recoveries.

Reduced glutathione (GSH), hydrogen tetrachloroaurate trihydrate (HAuCl4·3H2O), tetraethylorthosilicate (TEOS), ethylenediamine tetraacetic acid (EDTA), thiourea, cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB), ammonia, urea, leucine, HgCl2, FeCl3, CuCl2, AgNO3, Pb(NO3)2, CdCl2, CoCl2, and CrCl3 were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Company, Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The ultrapure water was from a Millipore water purification system. The solution of Hg2+ was prepared in 0.1 M HNO3, while the other metal ions were prepared in ultrapure water.

An F-7000 Spectrofluorometer (Hitachi) was adopted for fluorescence measurements. The morphology was characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2100 F). A Micromeritics ASAP 2020 Sorptometer (Micromeritics, ASAP 2020, USA) was used to measure determination of BET surface area.

Amino-modified CDs were prepared via the hydrothermal method using gelatin and 3-aminobenzeneboronic acid as the precursors [27]. Briefly, 0.6 g gelatin and 0.1 g of 3-aminobenzeneboronic acid were added to 30 mL ultrapure water, and a mixing solution was then sealed into a poly(tetrafluoroethylene)-lined autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 3 h. After centrifugation, the yellow supernatant was collected by centrifugation for the final CDs. Fluorescent Au NCs were synthesized according to the reported methods [28]. Typically, 5 mL of 20 mM freshly prepared HAuCl4 aqueous solution and 1.5 mL of 100 mM GSH were added to 44.5 mL ultrapure water. The mixture was heated to 70 °C for 24 h under general stirring to form gold nanoclusters.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of dual reference ion imprinted ratiometric fluorescence probes
ce:section.ce:para: Before polymerization, the fluorescence nanoparticles and the template were mixed to form a chelate. One milliliter amino-modified CDs and 0.1 mmol Ag+ were mixed to form the CDs-Ag+ complex, while 1 mL AuNCs and 0.1 mmol Pb2+ formed the AuNCs-Pb2+ complex. The mixture of 0.7 mL NaOH (2 M) and 100 mL of CTAB solution (5.5 × 10−3 M) was then heated to 70 °C followed by the addition of 1.0 mL of TEOS and the above template/monomer complex. The resulting mixture was stirred at 70 °C for 3 h. The precipitate was collected after the solution was cooled to room temperature. The 0.01 mM EDTA and 0.01 mM thiourea were selected as the lead and silver ions elution reagents. The ion imprinted fluorescence polymers were then rinsed five times to remove the redundant EDTA solution and thiourea via ultrapure water. Non-imprinted polymers (NIPs) as control materials were prepared using the same procedure but without template ions.

To detect Ag+ in buffer solutions, 10 μL of Ag+ solutions with different concentrations and the as-synthesized ion imprinted fluorescence polymers were mixed in 4.0 mL of PBS buffer solution (pH = 7.0, 10 mM). Before fluorescence measurements, the solutions were allowed to stand for 10 min in the dark at room temperature. To test selectivity, other metal ions (Co2+, Cr3+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Hg2+, Ag+, and Cu2+) were examined similarly. The Pb2+ ions were detected in buffer similar to Ag+ in buffer solutions. To simultaneously detect two kinds of ions, Ag+ and Pb2+ were added at different concentrations concurrently.

Real river water and tap water samples were used to investigate the practicality of the probes in real samples. Water was sampled from the Yi River (Linyi, Shandong) and filtered with a 0.22-μm membrane to remove solid suspensions. Tap water was obtained from the local drinking water and used directly without any pretreatment. Then water samples were spiked with Ag+ or Pb2+ at different concentrations. The content of Ag+ or Pb2+ in the water samples was analyzed using the above sensing system, and the recovery efficiency was calculated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of dual reference ion-imprinted ratiometric fluorescence probes
ce:section.ce:para: For the ion imprinted fluorescence polymers, fluorescent nanoparticles were randomly doped into the imprinted polymer. The conversion efficiency from molecular recognition to fluorescence signal output was inefficient. The functional groups provided by fluorescence nanoparticles for ion imprinting can effectively improve the conversion efficiency [13]. The fluorescence nanoparticles need to meet the following conditions. First, two fluorescence nanoparticles have a good resolution to ensure that two independent emission peaks appeared in the fluorescence spectrum. Second, fluorescent particles have specific functional groups that allow them to interact with the template molecules to form a stable complex. Furthermore, the interaction between template molecule and the functional groups cause significant changes in fluorescence intensity. Third, fluorescence nanoparticles only respond to one target. The presence of one target causes a change in one signal, and the other remains constant as a reference. Therefore, the two fluorescence nanoparticles were referenced to each other.

Here, CDs and AuNCs were employed, and their spectral performance was examined. The maximum emission peak of the CDs and AuNCs was at 430 and 600 nm, respectively. More importantly, the emission peak of the CDs does not coincide with the excitation peak of the AuNCs; thus, the two peaks are independent of each other (Fig. S1). The responsiveness of CDs and AuNCs to different metal ions was examined, and the results are listed in Fig. S1. The Ag+, Fe3+, and Hg2+ can cause a remarkable decrease in the fluorescence intensity of CDs, while Pb2+, Hg2+, and Cu2+ can quench AuNCs. The Hg2+ can simultaneously quench CDs and AuNCs and was not suitable as the detection target. The quenching effect of Cu2+ on Au NCs was weaker than Pb2+, while the effect of Fe3+ on CDs was weaker than Ag+. Considering the quenching behavior and quenching efficiency of various ions, Ag+ and Pb2+ were adopted as the detection targets. Thus, CDs and AuNCs were adopted as the fluorescence signal source, and Ag+ and Pb2+ templates were used to prepare dual-template ion imprinted ratiometric fluorescence probes with dual reference signals.

The mesoporous structure can effectively reduce mass transfer resistance, and thus mesoporous structured ion imprinted fluorescence polymers were prepared (Scheme 1 ). During the pre-polymerization process, CDs were mixed with Ag+, while AuNCs were mixed with Pb2+ to form functional group-template ions chelate complex. During the polymerization process, ion imprinted fluorescence polymers were prepared via a one-step doping method by directly adding functional group-template complexes to the polymerization solution. CTAB was used as a soft template to form mesoporous channels. After eluting the template molecules and CTAB micelles, mesoporous structured ion-imprinted fluorescence polymers were prepared.

The morphology of the prepared IIP and NIP was characterized by TEM and SEM (Fig. 1 ). The as-prepared ion imprinted fluorescence polymers have a uniform spherical morphology with rough surfaces; the particle size is about 100 nm. The mesoporous structure was confirmed by those worm-like channels and typical type IV nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherm curves. The surface area obtained from the nitrogen isotherms was 652 m2/g, and the average pore diameter calculated from the adsorption branch of the isotherms was 2.4 nm. The large specific surface area can increase the adsorption capacity, and the existence of mesoporous channels is advantageous for increases in the mass transfer rate.

FT-IR spectra of IIP and NIP were indicated in Fig. S2. The curve of IIP was similar to the curve of NIP because of the same raw materials. The characteristic peak around 3420 cm−1 corresponds to OH groups. The strong absorption peak around 1085 cm−1 and 806 cm−1 attributed to Si-O groups. Appearing absorbance peak at 1619 cm−1 corresponding to vibration of CO groups. The peak of –SH was extremely weak, it could not be observed. The location peak of –NH2 is at 3400−3100 cm−1 which was relatively weak while existing in the silica network, it also could not be observed.

The fluorescence spectra of the ion imprinted fluorescence polymers were characterized in Fig. 1D. Well-resolved dual emission bands were observed under a single wavelength excitation at 360 nm, which is attributed to the emission of CDs and AuNCs. When Ag+ was present, the CDs were quenched, while AuNCs remain unchanged as the reference. When Pb2+ was detected, AuNCs were quenched, while CDs remain unchanged as the reference. When both were present, both the CDs and AuNCs were quenched.

The chemical and photo-stability of the as-prepared ion-imprinted fluorescence polymers were examined against UV light, various anions, and pH (Fig. S3). The fluorescence intensity at 430 and 600 nm and the relative fluorescence intensity (I430/I600) have no apparent change after illumination with UV light at 365 nm for 120 min confirming the photostability of the ion-imprinted fluorescence polymers (Fig. S3A). The tolerance of the ion-imprinted fluorescence polymers to various anions was confirmed in Fig. S3B. The presence of nitrate, sulfate, phosphate, and chloride has no effect on the fluorescence intensity of CDs and AuNCs or the relative fluorescence intensity (I430/I600)—even the concentration of chloride can reach 20 mM. Fig. S3C shows that the fluorescence intensity remained stable between pH 6.0 and 8.0, and it reached a maximum value near 7.0. Thus, a pH = 7.0 was chosen for further experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of dual reference ion-imprinted fluorescence polymers
ce:section.ce:para: The amount of ion imprinted fluorescence polymers was first optimized to get the ideal detection performance. The fluorescence spectra of different concentrations of ion imprinted fluorescence polymers (e.g., 80, 100, and 150 mg L−1) (Fig.S4) to different concentration of Ag+ and Pb2+ were investigated, and Table S1 summarizes the linear range and limit of detection (LOD) of Ag+ and Pb2+. Through comprehensive consideration of both sensitivity and linear range, 100 mg L-1 of ion imprinted fluorescence polymers was adopted for detection of Ag+ and Pb2+ in the following experiment.

The fluorescence responses upon addition of different amounts of Ag+ and Pb2+ were measured to evaluate the sensitivity of the ion-imprinted fluorescence polymers. Upon addition of Ag+, the fluorescence intensity of CDs changed, while the intensity of AuNCs was constant (Fig. 2 A). Fig. 2B shows that (I600)0/I430 increases with increasing Ag+ concentration, and it was closely related to the amount of Ag+ from 0.2–12.5 μM with a detection limit as low as 86 nM based on 3 S/N (signal-to-noise). To facilitate the detection of two kinds of ions simultaneously, the fluorescence intensity of AuNCs at 600 nm of the blank sample recorded as (I600)0 was adopted for quantification. Since the intensity of the AuNCs remained basically unchanged, it did not affect the accuracy of the test results. The as-prepared probe could be applied for Ag+ detection in real water samples (the allowable level of Ag+ in drinking water set by the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency is ˜920 nM [29]).

Upon the addition of Pb2+, the fluorescence intensity of AuNCs was continuously quenched, whereas the intensity of CDs remains unchanged (Fig. 3 A). Fig. 3B indicates that (I430)0/I600 was closely related to the amount of Pb2+ ranging from 50 to 900 nM with a detection limit as low as 26 nM based on 3 S/N. Similarly, to facilitate the detection of two kinds of ions simultaneously in the following work, the fluorescence intensity of the blank sample at 430 nm (recorded as (I430)0) was adopted for quantification. Since the intensity of the CDs remains basically unchanged, it did not affect the accuracy of the test results. The U.S. Environmental Protection Agency has set the maximum allowable levels of lead in drinking water as 72 nM [30], and thus the probe is useful here.

The most important feature of ratiometric fluorescence probes is visual detection of targets. The fluorescence intensity changes at two wavelengths will result in significant fluorescent color changes. For visual detection, the fluorescence intensity changes at two wavelengths should stem from one target. Here, the main purpose was to explore the feasibility of simultaneous detection of two kinds of metal ions. The fluorescence intensity changes at two wavelengths would come from two different targets in complicated samples—this is not suitable for visual inspection, and thus visual detection is not discussed here.

The dose response of the non-imprinted fluorescence probe to Ag+ and Pb2+ was also examined, and the fluorescence spectra were recorded as Figs. 2C and 3 C. From the quenching constant (the slope of the standard curve) (Figs. 2D and 3 D), we can see that NIP displayed lower sensitivity than the corresponding IIP. This phenomenon can be attributed to a lack of recognition sites in the NIP. Imprinting factor, which can be calculated as the ratio of the quenching constant of the IIPs and NIPs, is an important index to evaluate the imprinting effect of the imprinted materials. The imprinted factor for Pb2+and Ag+ ions was 8.4 and 6.7, respectively. The higher imprinted factor displays a higher imprinting effect.

The IIP was used for simultaneous detection of Ag+ and Pb2+, and results are listed in Fig. 4 . Fig. 4A indicates that upon addition of Ag+ and Pb2+, the fluorescence intensity of CDs and AuNCs was simultaneously decreased. (I600)0/I430 was adopted for the quantification of Ag+. (I600)0 was the fluorescence intensity of AuNCs at 600 nm of blank sample, and (I430) was the fluorescence intensity of CDs at 430 nm exposed to different concentrations of Ag+. Similarly, (I430)0/I600 was adopted for quantification of Pb2+, in which (I430)0 was the fluorescence intensity of CDs at 430 nm of blank sample, and (I600) was the fluorescence intensity of AuNCs at 600 nm exposed to different concentrations of Pb2+. Fig. 4B and C show that the obtained linear range and detection limit were consistent with the individual detection data. The above results show that simultaneous detection of two ions was feasible by the dual reference IIP. The NIP was also used for simultaneous detection of Ag+ and Pb2+, and fluorescence spectra are shown in Fig. 4D. Fig. 4D shows that the fluorescence intensity changes have a trend that is similar for simultaneous detection of two ions as in single-ion detection. These results showed that the simultaneous detection of two targets can be achieved with a dual reference ratio fluorescent probe.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity studies for Ag+ and Pb2+ detection
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity of IIP was demonstrated via selective binding test. The fluorescence spectra of the IIP in the presence of several metal ions including Cd2+, Pb2+, Ag+, Fe3+, Cu2+, Co2+, Cr3+, and Hg2+ were recorded under identical conditions (Fig. 5 A). Fig. S1B and C show that Cd2+, Co2+, and Cr3+ did not affect the fluorescence intensity of CDs and AuNCs. In the ion imprinted polymers, the existence of Cd2+, Co2+, and Cr3+ did not impact the intensity of CDs and AuNCs. Compared with the blank sample, the fluorescence intensity of AuNCs decreased in the presence of template Pb2+; when template Ag+ ions were present, the fluorescence intensity of CDs at 430 nm decreases. The Hg2+ and Cu2+ can quench AuNCs, while Fe3+ and Hg2+ can quench CDs (Fig.S1B and C). Interestingly, the interference of Hg2+ and Cu2+ on Pb2+ detection can be removed, and the interference of Fe3+ and Hg2+ on Ag+ detection also can be eliminated after the imprinting process.

The fluorescence intensity of NIP did not obviously change when exposed to Cd2+, Pb2+, Ag+, Fe3+, Cu2+, Co2+, Cr3+, and Hg2+ ions—even when also exposed to Pb2+ and Ag+ (Fig. 5B). I430/I600 for various ions is shown in Fig. 5C. For Cd2+, Fe3+, Cu2+, Co2+, Cr3+, and Hg2+, I430/I600 was substantially unchanged. For Pb2+, I430/I600 was sharply increased but was much lower for Ag+. IIP displayed good selectivity for Pb2+ and Ag+, and the interference of other metal ions can be removed by ion imprinting. This good selectivity comes from the imprinting process. For template ions, there are recognition sites that match the size, shape, and functional groups to the template inside the imprinted polymers. When the templates were added to the detection system, they can rapidly diffuse and be adsorbed to the recognition sites, thereby causing an apparent change in the fluorescence intensity of fluorescence nanoparticles. Pb(II), Cu(II), and Hg(II) have the same charge, and the higher selectivity of IIP for Pb(II) might be attributed to different chelation forces between the sulfur and nitrogen atoms on the AuNCs surface and various ions. Ag (I), Fe (III), and Hg (II) displayed large differences in charge and radius; thus, the size of the recognition sites would be different. The affinities between the functional groups on CDs surface and various ions show big differences.

To further investigate the selectivity of IIP for Pb2+ and Ag+ detection, IIP was exposed to template ions in the presence of various competitive metal ions, and the fluorescence spectra were recorded and are displayed in Fig. 5D. Compared with the detection line of Ag+, Ag++Hg2+, and Ag++Fe3+, the presence of coexisting ions has little effect on the fluorescence intensity. Similarly, the existence of Cu2+ and Hg2+ did not cause obvious interference.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Practical applications in real water samples
ce:section.ce:para: Next, Pb2+ and Ag+ were measured in real water samples to investigate the practical utility of this method. In view of the difference between the actual water sample and the buffer solution, the fluorescence spectra and linear fitting curve of the real sample were supplied as Figs. S5 and S6. No Pb2+ was detected in tap water samples, while the estimated concentration of Pb2+ in river water was 96 nM. Ag+ was not detected in river water or tap water samples. Spiking experiments added Pb2+ and Ag+ simultaneously to water samples, and (I430)0/I600 or (I600)0/I430 was adopted to measure Pb2+ or Ag+. The estimated recoveries and the RSD are listed in Table 1 . Ideal recoveries were obtained for spiking experiments. The spiking experimental results show that the dual reference IIFP can be employed to simultaneously detect Pb2+ and Ag+. This probe will simplify the metal ions analysis in water samples.

In this paper, a dual reference IIP was first proposed for the simultaneous detection of Pb2+ or Ag+ in water samples. The method developed here offers high selectivity, high sensitivity, and high throughput detection. The advantage of high selectivity comes from the ion imprinting process, which eliminates the interference of Cu2+, Hg2+, and Fe3+ ions. Detection sensitivity was improved through mesoporous structure and ratio signal output. The Pb2+ ions linear range was 50―900 nM with a detection limit of 26 nM. The Ag+ ion linear range was 0.2―12.5 μM with a detection limit of 86 nM. More importantly, simultaneous detection of two kinds of targets with fluorescence signal output was achieved by selecting the appropriate fluorophore. The as-prepared IIP was successfully applied for simultaneous detection of Ag+ ions and Pb2+ ions in river and tap water samples to greatly reduce the cost. The actual water samples recovery rate was 94.6―110.5% with RSDs of 3.2―4.6%. The implementation of this strategy brings innovation to the signal output mode in the field of imprinting sensors while also offering high-throughput fluorescence detection.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21777065).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2019.03.069.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
